Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd say I didn't really have a set schedule - I just kind of studied when I felt like it or when something was due soon. Some weeks I'd put in decent hours, maybe cramming a lot into a couple of days, and other weeks I'd barely touch anything. I learn better by doing stuff with my hands anyway, so sitting down to read or watch lectures felt like a struggle most of the time.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. Deadlines were the main thing that got me moving - if something was due, I'd suddenly find the motivation to study. Otherwise, I'd drift and do other things. I had some weeks where I tried to be more consistent, but life always seemed to get in the way, and I'd fall back into my random patterns.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline coming up or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes my peers would message me about an assignment and I'd realize I needed to check what was going on. I'd also log in when I was bored and just scroll through, not really doing much productive. The peer support group helped remind me to check in sometimes too.
Q4: There were a few weeks where I got into a rhythm of logging in regularly, mostly because I was chatting with classmates through the platform. The social aspect made it feel less like a chore. But that routine never really stuck for long - I'd get distracted by other stuff or just lose interest, and then I'd go days without logging in until something urgent came up.
Q5: The discussion boards were actually pretty useful for me because I could interact with other students and figure out what was actually important. When someone explained something in their own words, it clicked better than the lectures. I also liked any hands-on activities or simulations when they were available - those matched how I actually learn. The peer interactions kept me somewhat connected to the course.
Q6: I barely watched the lecture videos, to be honest. I'd start them and then zone out after a few minutes - they just didn't hold my attention. The readings I mostly ignored too unless I absolutely needed them for an assignment. Anything that felt like passive consumption, I'd put off or skip entirely. I know I should've engaged more, but it just felt pointless sitting there watching someone talk.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly helped me pass or if it involved actually doing something rather than just absorbing information. If I could work with other students on it, that helped too. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to the grade, I'd usually skip. I needed to feel like my effort was going somewhere concrete.
Q8: Optional activities basically didn't exist for me. If it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it - I had limited energy and resources, so I had to prioritize. Required stuff at least had consequences if I didn't do it, which gave me some motivation. Optional things just felt like extra work that wouldn't pay off, so they got pushed aside every time.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download materials and then study them on my phone during commutes or when I didn't have good internet at home. My resources are pretty limited, so I couldn't always be online when I wanted to study. I'd also work through problems on paper or practice things physically when I could, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going over notes I'd written out by hand or working through practice problems offline. I'd discuss concepts with friends in person or over text, which helped me understand stuff better than any video could. None of that shows up in the LMS. Sometimes I'd be genuinely trying to learn but just not in ways the platform could track.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or save some slides, then never open them in the LMS again. I'd study from those files on my phone or print them out when I could afford to. The LMS would show I accessed something once, but I might have reviewed it multiple times offline. It was just easier that way given my internet situation.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially around midterms when everything piled up at once. I'd feel this wave of stress and then kind of shut down instead of pushing through. When I couldn't see immediate progress or when assignments felt overwhelming, I'd just disengage and tell myself I'd catch up later. Sometimes life stuff - money worries, family things - made it hard to focus on school at all.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from other responsibilities, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Confusion about what was actually required made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face how behind I was. When I had competing stuff going on - work, helping family - coursework always took a backseat. The fatigue was real, and it showed in how little I engaged sometimes.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd open a video and let it play while I was doing something else, or I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere even though technically I was 'engaging' with the content. It was like going through the motions without actually learning. Those sessions felt pretty hollow.
Q15: I postponed stuff constantly, even when I knew it mattered. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed - if something seemed too big, I'd avoid it entirely. Low resilience, I guess. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd push it again. The anxiety about doing poorly made me avoid starting in the first place, which is kind of backwards but that's how my brain works.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly kick into gear, cramming everything at the last minute. Without that pressure, I'd just drift. The LMS became much more active for me in the hours before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only system that worked for me.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it was surprising - sometimes better than I expected, which motivated me a bit. But honestly, I didn't always check feedback promptly, so it didn't always influence my next steps. Positive feedback made me feel like maybe I could do this, but it wasn't enough to change my overall habits. The surprise of doing okay sometimes kept me from completely giving up.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it didn't really match how I learn best. I needed more hands-on stuff and less passive watching. When modules were clearly laid out with specific deadlines, I could at least plan my cramming sessions. But when things were vague or spread out, I'd lose track completely. More structured peer activities would have helped me stay connected.
Q19: The lack of kinesthetic or interactive elements made it harder for me to stay engaged. I learn by doing, not by watching or reading. When there were opportunities to work with peers or do something practical, I was more into it. The heavy reliance on videos and readings worked against my learning style. Also, having limited resources meant some design choices assumed access I didn't always have.
Q20: I think that's pretty relatable actually. You might be logging in, clicking around, trying to figure things out, but not actually producing work. Maybe you're confused about requirements, or stressed, or just not in the right headspace to complete things. High activity could mean someone's struggling to understand what they need to do, not that they're successfully engaging.
Q21: That makes sense to me. If someone's studying offline or has efficient ways of learning outside the system, their LMS activity would be low but their understanding could be solid. I did okay on my exam and assignments when I actually submitted them, even though my attendance and LMS activity were pretty weak. The platform doesn't capture everything about how someone learns.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're logging in and what you're clicking on, but they miss the quality of that engagement completely. They can't tell if you're actually understanding something or just going through motions. They definitely miss offline studying, peer conversations, and all the mental work that happens away from the screen. It's a pretty limited picture of what's really going on.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort to understand it, not just checking boxes. For me, it's when I'm actively thinking about the material, asking questions, connecting it to real stuff. It's not about how many times you log in - it's about whether your brain is actually involved. I wasn't always engaged even when I was technically participating.
Q24: The data wouldn't show how much I was struggling with motivation and feeling overwhelmed, or how my limited resources affected my access. It wouldn't capture the peer conversations that actually helped me learn, or the offline studying I did. It also wouldn't show that I was genuinely surprised sometimes by how things turned out - like I cared more than my activity suggested, I just couldn't always translate that into consistent engagement.
